title: DC-BUN-003: Full Bun workspace migration
body:
**Epic**: #163 Bun Migration Path
**Iteration**: TBD
**Priority**: P3

## Description

Complete migration from pnpm workspaces to Bun workspaces as the primary package manager and runtime. Set `packageManager: "bun@x.x"` in root package.json. Evaluate replacing Turborepo with native Bun build.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Migrate `pnpm-workspace.yaml` → Bun workspace configuration
- [ ] Set `packageManager: "bun@x.x"` in root `package.json`
- [ ] Evaluate and document whether Turborepo can be replaced by native Bun build
- [ ] All packages build, test, lint, and typecheck successfully
- [ ] CI pipeline fully migrated to Bun
- [ ] Remove pnpm configuration files if no longer needed

## Trigger Condition

This task should only be started when Bun workspaces have:
- Graph-based filtering (not just glob)
- Stable `workspace:*` protocol
- Proven production monorepo adoption

Expected: Bun v3/v4

## Notes

- Phase 3 (final) of Bun migration
- Depends on DC-BUN-001 and DC-BUN-002 being complete
- Total CI improvement target: ~53s → ~14s

title: DC-BUN-002: Replace pnpm install with bun install in CI
body:
**Epic**: #163 Bun Migration Path
**Iteration**: TBD
**Priority**: P3

## Description

Replace `pnpm install` with `bun install` in CI pipeline once Bun's `workspace:*` protocol is stable (expected Bun v2.x).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] CI workflow uses `bun install` instead of `pnpm install`
- [ ] CI workflow uses `bun test` instead of `vitest run` (prerequisite: DC-BUN-001)
- [ ] Keep `tsc --noEmit` for type checking
- [ ] Keep Turborepo for task orchestration
- [ ] All workspace dependencies resolve correctly with `bun install`
- [ ] Verify `workspace:*` protocol works reliably

## Trigger Condition

This task should only be started when `workspace:*` protocol is stable in Bun (Bun v2.x).

## Notes

- Phase 2 of Bun migration — install + test
- Benefit: ~10x faster installs (~18s → ~6s) + ~10x faster tests
- Depends on DC-BUN-001 being complete

title: DC-PROV-002: Minimax Token API Provider
body:
## Context

The DiriCode Provider Router (Epic #3) needs to support Minimax as an additional AI provider. This would add a third provider option alongside the existing Copilot (priority 1) and Kimi (priority 2).

## Requirements

1. **API Integration**
   - Integrate with Minimax's Token API (moapi.io)
   - Support chat completion and streaming endpoints
   - Handle Minimax-specific authentication (API key + group ID)

2. **Provider Implementation**
   - Implement `Provider` interface from `@diricode/providers`
   - Follow existing patterns used for Copilot/Kimi adapters
   - Support streaming via Minimax's SSE endpoint

3. **Configuration**
   - Add Minimax to `ProviderPriorities` (priority 3)
   - Support `DC_MINIMAX_API_KEY` and `DC_MINIMAX_GROUP_ID` env vars
   - Model selection: `abab6.5s-chat`, `abab6.5g-chat`, etc.

4. **Documentation**
   - Document Minimax API setup and rate limits
   - Add Minimax to the provider comparison table

## Labels

- `feature`
- `component:providers`
- `area:provider-registry`
- `conflict:shared-types`
- `type:enhancement`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Minimax provider implements the `Provider` interface
- [ ] Streaming and non-streaming completions work
- [ ] Error handling for invalid API key / quota exceeded
- [ ] Priority registration in registry (priority 3)
- [ ] Unit tests for Minimax adapter

## Dependencies

Depends on: #27 (Provider interface/registry) — Completed

title: DC-WEB-007: Work mode selector (4 dimensions) [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #17 Web UI MVP [MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Work mode control panel: 4 sliders (Quality 0–3, Autonomy 0–3, Verbose 0–2, Creativity 0–3). Live labels per value. Persisted to session config. Emits config.workMode.changed event.

title: DC-WEB-006: Approval dialog [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #17 Web UI MVP [MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Modal approval dialog triggered by approval_required events. Shows: action description, risk level, file/command details. Approve/Deny buttons. 60s countdown auto-deny.

title: DC-WEB-005: Session management UI [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #17 Web UI MVP [MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Session list sidebar: create new session, resume past session, delete session. Session metadata display (created_at, status, token count). Search/filter.

title: DC-WEB-004: File diff viewer [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #17 Web UI MVP [MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Inline diff viewer for file changes emitted via EventStream. Side-by-side and unified modes. Syntax highlighting. Accept/reject controls (fires approval event).

title: DC-WEB-003: Chat interface [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #17 Web UI MVP [MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Message thread with user/assistant/system message types. Streaming message display (token-by-token). Markdown rendering with code highlighting. Input box with submit and interrupt controls.

title: DC-WEB-002: SSE client and global state management [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #17 Web UI MVP [MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

SSE client connecting to /api/v1/events. Zustand global store for session, agent tree, metrics. Reconnect logic with exponential backoff. Connection status indicator.

title: DC-WEB-001: Vite + React project scaffold [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #17 Web UI MVP [MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Vite + React 18 + TypeScript scaffold in packages/web. Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui setup. Dev proxy to Hono server (port 3000). Build output to dist/.

title: DC-OBS-006: Live Activity Indicator [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #16 EventStream Observability Backbone [MVP-2 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Animated activity indicator showing current agent + current tool call. Transitions: idle → thinking → tool_call → streaming → complete. Dismissible on click.

title: DC-OBS-005: Metrics Bar component [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #16 EventStream Observability Backbone [MVP-2 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Persistent bottom bar showing: total tokens, estimated cost, active agent count, pipeline phase, elapsed time. Updates live via EventStream SSE.

title: DC-OBS-004: Agent Tree component [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #16 EventStream Observability Backbone [MVP-2 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

React component visualizing live agent delegation tree: parent-child hierarchy, per-agent status (active/complete/error), token usage, depth indicators.

title: DC-OBS-003: Metrics aggregation engine [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #16 EventStream Observability Backbone [MVP-2 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Subscribe to EventStream, maintain real-time metrics: token usage, cost, active agents, tool call counts, error rates, pipeline phase durations. Sliding window aggregation.

title: DC-OBS-002: Event emission integration [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #16 EventStream Observability Backbone [MVP-2 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Instrument all core execution paths with EventStream emission: agent lifecycle, tool calls, pipeline phases, delegation, approval requests, errors.

title: DC-OBS-001: EventStream core implementation [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #16 EventStream Observability Backbone [MVP-2 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Typed EventStream bus: publish(event), subscribe(filter, handler), unsubscribe. Event envelope: id, type, timestamp, sessionId, agentId?, payload. In-memory fan-out.

title: DC-SKILL-006: Work mode integration (4 dimensions) [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #15 Skills System [MVP-2 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Skill activation/deactivation based on 4 work-mode dimensions: Quality (0–3), Autonomy (0–3), Verbose (0–2), Creativity (0–3). Dimension thresholds per skill.

title: DC-SKILL-005: Built-in skill: frontend-specialist [MVP-3]

Built-in frontend-specialist skill: React/TypeScript patterns, Tailwind conventions, accessibility requirements, component composition guidelines.

title: DC-SKILL-004: Built-in skill: git-master [MVP-3]
body:

**Iteration**: MVP-3

Built-in git-master skill: safe git workflow guidance, atomic commit patterns, branch strategy, conflict resolution heuristics. Injected for git-operator agent.

title: DC-SKILL-003: Skill injection into agent prompts [MVP-2]

**Iteration**: MVP-2

Inject applicable skills into agent prompt builder: append systemPromptAddition sections, merge toolset constraints, apply work-mode-conditional skills.

title: DC-SKILL-002: Skill loader and registry [MVP-2]

**Iteration**: MVP-2

Load skills from built-in directory + project .dc/skills/. Registry: register, getByName, listByTag, listForWorkMode. Validation on load with error reporting.

title: DC-SKILL-001: Skill definition interface [MVP-2]

**Epic**: #15 Skills System [MVP-2 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

SkillDefinition type: id, name, description, systemPromptAddition, toolset (allow/deny lists), constraints, workModeConditions. Zod schema for validation.

title: DC-HOOK-005: Hook configuration (config.jsonc) [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #14 Hook Framework and MVP Hook Set [MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Hook configuration schema in DiriCodeConfig: hooks array with type, handler (in-process module or shell command), enabled flag, timeout override, event filter.

title: DC-HOOK-004: MVP hook implementations (6 hooks) [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #14 Hook Framework and MVP Hook Set [MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Implement 6 MVP hooks: session-start, pre-commit, post-commit, error-retry, plan-created, plan-validated. Each hook has typed payload and documented contract.

title: DC-HOOK-003: External hook executor [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #14 Hook Framework and MVP Hook Set [MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Spawn external process hooks with 10s timeout, stdin/stdout JSON protocol, process isolation, resource cleanup on timeout/error.

title: DC-HOOK-002: In-process hook executor [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #14 Hook Framework and MVP Hook Set [MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

TypeScript in-process hook execution (<3ms target). Synchronous and async hook support. Context injection (session, config, event payload). Result validation.

title: DC-HOOK-001: Hook engine and lifecycle [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #14 Hook Framework and MVP Hook Set [MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Hook registration, discovery, and execution lifecycle. Hooks declared in config.jsonc. Fail-isolated: hook errors never crash orchestration. Emit hook.start/complete/error events.

title: DC-CTX-009: Active file tracking [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #13 Context Management [MVP-1 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Track recently read/written files per session. Boost their rank in context scoring. Configurable recency window and decay function.

title: DC-CTX-008: Symbols FTS5 search [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #13 Context Management [MVP-1 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

FTS5 index over symbol names and docstrings. Ranked symbol search returning file + line + kind + snippet. Used by context composer for relevance-based file inclusion.

title: DC-CTX-007: Context composer with budget fitting [MVP-3]
body:
**Epic**: #13 Context Management [MVP-1 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-3

Adaptive token allocator: partition budget across repo-map/history/files/instructions. Binary-search fitting to stay within model limit. Priority ordering: instructions > recent history > repo map > files.

title: DC-CTX-006: convoSummary condenser [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #13 Context Management [MVP-1 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Sliding window conversation summarizer. Compress older turns to summaries (summarizer agent). Preserve last N turns verbatim. Trigger at configurable token threshold.

title: DC-CTX-005: observationMasking condenser [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #13 Context Management [MVP-1 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Mask resolved observations (completed tasks, superseded plans) to reduce context noise. Preserve outcome summaries. Configurable masking aggressiveness.

title: DC-CTX-004: fileReadDedup condenser [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #13 Context Management [MVP-1 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Detect and deduplicate repeated file reads in conversation history. Replace redundant reads with `[file: X already shown above]` reference tokens.

title: DC-CTX-003: Repo map generator [MVP-1]
body:
**Epic**: #13 Context Management [MVP-1 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-1

Token-budget-aware repo map: directory tree + top-ranked files + public API signatures. Supports full/condensed/minimal modes. Fits within configurable token limit.

title: DC-CTX-002: File rank scoring (PageRank) [MVP-1]
body:
**Epic**: #13 Context Management [MVP-1 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-1

Import graph construction + PageRank scoring per file. Scores cached in SQLite. Incremental recalculation on file change. Used to rank context candidates.

title: DC-CTX-001: Structural index with tree-sitter [MVP-1]
body:
**Epic**: #13 Context Management [MVP-1 → MVP-3]
**Iteration**: MVP-1

Tree-sitter based symbol extraction for TypeScript/JavaScript. Symbols stored in SQLite with file, line, kind, name. Incremental update on file change.

title: DC-PIPE-008: Deviation rules [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #12 Pipeline Orchestrator and Execution Control [MVP-1 → MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Detect plan deviation during execution. Low-risk: auto-approve. High-risk: pause + user approval (DC-SAFE-005). Deviation event emission.

title: DC-PIPE-007: Context budget enforcement [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #12 Pipeline Orchestrator and Execution Control [MVP-1 → MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Pre-turn context size estimation, enforce token budget per phase, trigger context compression (DC-CTX) when approaching limit, abort turn if compression insufficient.

title: DC-PIPE-006: Analysis paralysis guard [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #12 Pipeline Orchestrator and Execution Control [MVP-1 → MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Detect excessive planning loops (>3 plan iterations without execute progress). Auto-escalate to user. Configurable threshold.

title: DC-PIPE-005: Wave-based parallel execution [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #12 Pipeline Orchestrator and Execution Control [MVP-1 → MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Dependency-graph based wave scheduler: tasks with no deps run in parallel, dependent tasks wait. Wave result aggregation, partial wave failure handling.

title: DC-PIPE-004: Interview→Plan→Execute→Verify pipeline [MVP-2]
body:
**Epic**: #12 Pipeline Orchestrator and Execution Control [MVP-1 → MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-2

Full 4-phase pipeline. Plan phase: planner-thorough agent. Verify phase: verifier agent. Plan persistence + re-use on resume. Phase skip rules.

title: DC-PIPE-003: Sequential task execution [MVP-1]
body:
**Epic**: #12 Pipeline Orchestrator and Execution Control [MVP-1 → MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-1

Task queue with sequential execution, per-task result capture, error propagation rules (abort vs. continue), partial completion recovery.

title: DC-PIPE-002: Basic Interview→Execute flow [MVP-1]
body:
**Epic**: #12 Pipeline Orchestrator and Execution Control [MVP-1 → MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-1

2-phase pipeline: Interview (intent clarification) → Execute (agent delegation). Dispatcher agent as Execute coordinator. Memory checkpoint after each phase.

title: DC-PIPE-001: Turn lifecycle [MVP-1]
body:
**Epic**: #12 Pipeline Orchestrator and Execution Control [MVP-1 → MVP-2]
**Iteration**: MVP-1
**Package**: `@diricode/core`
**Spec**: `docs/mvp/epic-pipeline.md` → DC-PIPE-001

## Goal

Create a robust turn execution lifecycle with traceable IDs, timeout safety, and result consolidation. A "turn" is the atomic unit of interaction — one user message in, one consolidated result out, with full telemetry tracking.

## Scope

- **Lifecycle flow**:
  1. User message received
  2. `turn.start` event emitted (with unique `turn_id`)
  3. Dispatch to execution path (direct or planned)
  4. Agent execution + tool actions (all tagged with `turn_id`)
  5. `turn.end` emitted with result summary
- **Turn identity**:
  - Unique `turn_id` (ULID or UUID v7 for time-ordering)
  - Propagated across dispatcher → agents → tools → memory writes as correlation ID
- **Timeout policy**:
  - Configurable turn timeout (default **5 minutes**)
  - Clean timeout state transition with typed error result
  - Partial results preserved on timeout (don't discard work done)
- **Result aggregation**:
  - Collect: outputs, status, cost/tokens, key observations, files changed
  - Produce deterministic turn result envelope
  - Store in SQLite `turns` table via memory layer

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Every user message creates exactly one turn with a unique ID
- [ ] `turn.start` and `turn.end` events are always emitted (including failure/timeout paths)
- [ ] Timeout is enforced with default 5-minute cap and config override
- [ ] Turn result includes aggregated status, outputs, and telemetry references
- [ ] Turn ID is available in memory timeline and observability events
- [ ] Turn ID propagates as correlation ID through all agent/tool invocations
- [ ] Partial results are preserved when timeout fires (graceful degradation)

## Turn State Machine

```
idle → processing → streaming → complete
                  ↘             ↗
                    → error
                  ↘
                    → timeout
```

## Schema Sketch (for `turns` table in SQLite)

```sql
CREATE TABLE turns (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,           -- ULID or UUID v7
  session_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id),
  status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'processing' CHECK(status IN ('processing','streaming','complete','error','timeout')),
  input TEXT NOT NULL,           -- user message content
  output TEXT,                   -- consolidated result
  tokens_in INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
  tokens_out INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
  cost REAL DEFAULT 0.0,
  started_at TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
  completed_at TEXT,
  timeout_ms INTEGER DEFAULT 300000,  -- 5 min default
  metadata TEXT DEFAULT '{}'     -- JSON: agent_ids, files_changed, observations_count
);

CREATE INDEX idx_turns_session ON turns(session_id, started_at);
```

## Implementation Notes

### Mandatory Web Research Before Implementation

Research the following before starting:

1. **Microsoft Agent Framework turn pipeline** — Study the turn execution pipeline: input processing → middleware → LLM invocation → tool execution → state persistence. Key insight: "state loads at turn start and persists at turn end" — turn is the transaction boundary. See [interwebicly.com/blog/microsoft-agent-framework-internals](https://interwebicly.com/blog/microsoft-agent-framework-internals)
2. **OpenAI Agents SDK turn tracking** — Study `AdvancedSQLiteSession`'s `turn_usage` table that tracks `requests`, `input_tokens`, `output_tokens`, `total_tokens` per turn with JSON details. `user_turn_number` provides deterministic ordering
3. **Correlation ID patterns** — Study distributed tracing patterns (OpenTelemetry trace/span IDs) for propagating turn ID through async agent/tool chains. See W3C Trace Context spec
4. **Turn timeout and cancellation** — Study how Semantic Kernel handles turn-level timeouts (`turn timeout: how long the entire turn can run including all LLM calls and tool executions`) and the distinction between tool-level vs turn-level timeouts
5. **Concurrent turn isolation** — Study Semantic Kernel issue ([microsoft/semantic-kernel#13639](https://github.com/microsoft/semantic-kernel/issues/13639)) on dialogue state corruption under concurrent multi-turn conversations. Key lesson: state must be keyed by conversation ID with proper isolation

### Reference Implementations

- **Microsoft Agent Framework**: Turn pipeline with discrete phases (input processing → middleware → LLM invocation → tool execution → state persistence). State persistence is synchronous — turn doesn't complete until state is written. Turn timeout is the hard limit. Latency breakdown: state load (50-200ms), LLM call (2-15s), tool execution (variable), state save (50-200ms)
- **OpenAI Agents SDK**: `turn_usage` table with per-turn token analytics, branch support (`branch_id`), and `user_turn_number` for deterministic ordering. `message_structure` table tracks message types and sequence numbers within each turn
- **Semantic Kernel Process Framework**: Turn state machine with explicit phase transitions. Demonstrated isolation bugs when concurrent conversations share agent instances — validates need for strict session-scoped turn state
- **AutoGen**: Conversation loop model where tool calls execute atomically within a turn. `max_turns` parameter controls execution depth. Single-turn mode (`max_turns=1`) enables external orchestration between turns

### Existing Code Context

- Dispatcher at `packages/agents/src/dispatcher.ts` — currently processes requests without turn tracking
- Server at `packages/server/src/server.ts` — Hono routes receive user messages, need to create turn on each request
- Event system not yet formalized — `turn.start`/`turn.end` events need event type definitions in `@diricode/core`
- Config schema at `packages/core/src/config/schema.ts` — add `pipeline.turnTimeoutMs` config option

## Dependencies

- **Upstream**: DC-MEM-002 (sessions — turns live within sessions)
- **Downstream**: DC-PIPE-002 (Interview→Execute flow uses turn lifecycle)
- **Downstream**: DC-PIPE-003 (Sequential execution emits per-task turns)
- **Cross-epic**: `epic-memory` (turn results persisted to SQLite)
- **Cross-epic**: `epic-observability` (turn events power metrics bar and activity indicator)

## References

- `docs/mvp/epic-pipeline.md` (canonical spec)
- `spec-mvp-diricode.md` (pipeline + telemetry expectations)
- `cross-cutting.md` (event naming, correlation IDs)

title: DC-MEM-007: Multi-project support [MVP-1]
body:
**Epic**: #11 Memory and Project State Backbone [MVP-1]
**Iteration**: MVP-1
**Package**: `@diricode/memory`
**Spec**: `docs/mvp/epic-memory.md` → DC-MEM-007

## Goal

Ensure strict per-project memory isolation with optional global cross-project memory. Each project gets its own `.dc/memory.db`, while a global `~/.config/dc/memory.db` stores cross-project observations and telemetry.

## Scope

- **Project-scoped DB**:
  - Each project uses its own `.dc/memory.db` at the project root
  - Project root detection via git root (`git rev-parse --show-toplevel`) or explicit config
- **Global DB**:
  - `~/.config/dc/memory.db` for cross-project observations and telemetry
  - Stores aggregated token usage, model quality scores, and cross-project search data
- **Project identity**:
  - Canonical project key derived from absolute normalized path
  - Consistent across symlinks and worktrees
- **Isolation rules**:
  - Project APIs default to project DB only
  - Cross-project reads require explicit opt-in context (no accidental bleed-through)
- **Data routing strategy**:
  - **Project DB**: sessions, messages, observations, issues, task state
  - **Global DB**: token usage aggregates, model scores, A/B experiment results, project registry
  - Conflict/duplication policy: project DB is source of truth, global DB stores derived aggregates

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Opening two projects yields isolated memory stores by default
- [ ] Global DB path and initialization are deterministic on supported OSes (macOS, Linux)
- [ ] Project identification uses canonical absolute paths (resolved through symlinks)
- [ ] Cross-project queries require explicit API usage (no accidental bleed-through)
- [ ] Worktree/multi-project workflows behave consistently with state isolation guarantees
- [ ] Data routing strategy is explicit and documented — which tables go where
- [ ] Global DB gracefully handles concurrent access from multiple DiriCode instances

## Architecture

```
~/.config/dc/
  memory.db          ← Global DB (project registry, model scores, usage aggregates)

/path/to/project-a/
  .dc/
    memory.db        ← Project A's sessions, observations, issues

/path/to/project-b/
  .dc/
    memory.db        ← Project B's sessions, observations, issues
```

## Implementation Notes

### Mandatory Web Research Before Implementation

Research the following before starting:

1. **Workspace-based memory isolation** — Study the `claude-mem` feature request ([thedotmack/claude-mem#1318](https://github.com/thedotmack/claude-mem/issues/1318)) for workspace-based SQLite isolation. Key patterns: per-workspace DB files, automatic workspace detection from CWD, graceful fallback to global DB, `WorkspaceDatabaseManager` for multi-DB connections
2. **SQLite ATTACH DATABASE** — Study SQLite's `ATTACH DATABASE` command for cross-DB queries when needed. A single library connection can manage multiple database files transparently. Transactions can span attached databases atomically
3. **Git root detection** — Study how tools detect project root: `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`, fallback to `.dc/` directory presence, then CWD. Handle edge cases: bare repos, worktrees, detached HEAD
4. **XDG Base Directory Specification** — Follow XDG for global config path: `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/dc/` (default `~/.config/dc/`). Study how tools like `gh` CLI and `pnpm` handle global state directories cross-platform

### Reference Implementations

- **claude-mem Workspace Isolation** (#1318): Proposed architecture with `CLAUDE_MEM_WORKSPACE_ROOTS` env var, per-workspace SQLite DBs, shared context within workspace, complete isolation between workspaces. Alternatives considered: per-project DBs (too granular), project filtering only (semantic search crosses boundaries), multiple installations (too complex)
- **SQLite Multi-Database**: `ATTACH DATABASE` enables joins across databases as if they were part of the same logical schema. One library connection internally manages multiple database connections. Transactions span attached databases atomically
- **VS Code Workspaces**: Multi-root workspace architecture with per-folder settings, shared workspace settings, and extension isolation. Demonstrates the per-project + global state hierarchy pattern
- **Panex**: WAL mode enables concurrent reads from one DB while writes happen from daemon. Validates concurrent access patterns for global DB

### Existing Code Context

- SQLite client at `packages/memory/src/db/client.ts` — currently opens a single DB connection
- Must be extended to support opening project DB + global DB with connection management
- Existing `ModelScoreRepository` and `ABExperimentManager` in `packages/providers/` — these write to global DB (model quality is cross-project)
- Config schema at `packages/core/src/config/schema.ts` — may need project root resolution logic

## Dependencies

- **Upstream**: DC-MEM-001 (SQLite setup) — ✅ implemented
- **Upstream**: DC-MEM-002 + DC-MEM-003 (sessions, observations — the data being isolated)
- **Cross-epic**: `epic-config` (project root detection, global config paths)
- **Cross-epic**: `epic-providers` (model scores write to global DB)

## References

- `docs/mvp/epic-memory.md` (canonical spec)
- `ankieta-features-ekosystem.md` (multi-project/worktree decision)
- `spec-mvp-diricode.md` (multi-project memory setting)
- `overview.md` (memory and package architecture)

title: DC-MEM-006: Local Issue System Client [MVP-1]
body:
**Epic**: #11 Memory and Project State Backbone [MVP-1]
**Iteration**: MVP-1
**Package**: `@diricode/memory`
**Spec**: `docs/mvp/epic-memory.md` → DC-MEM-006

## Goal

Deliver a local-first issue management interface backed by SQLite for managing tasks, epics, and their relationships. All runtime issue state is stored locally — no network dependency, no rate limits, sub-millisecond reads.

## Scope

- **Interface**: Define `IIssueClient` (backend-agnostic abstraction)
  - Must be testable via mocks/stubs without SQLite
  - Consumed by `@diricode/core` and pipeline through interface contracts only
- **SQLite-native adapter** implementing `IIssueClient`:
  - `createIssue()` — INSERT into local SQLite
  - `updateIssue()` — UPDATE local SQLite row
  - `closeIssue()` — UPDATE status in local SQLite
  - `listIssues()` — SELECT with filters (status, labels, assignee, parent)
  - `searchIssues()` — FTS5 full-text search over issue titles and descriptions
- **Epic support**:
  - Parent issue → child issue relationships via foreign keys
  - Parent-child traceability via `parent_id` column (not checklist text parsing)
  - Recursive queries for epic → all descendants
- **Offline-first guarantees**:
  - All operations write and read only from local SQLite
  - No network connection is required or checked for any state operation
- **Sync adapter boundary**:
  - Sync adapters (GitHub, GitLab, Jira) are output targets — they receive state changes from the local issue system
  - Adapters are planned for v2+ (see ADR-048)
  - No sync adapter logic in this issue — clean separation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `IIssueClient` abstraction is backend-agnostic and testable via mocks
- [ ] SQLite adapter supports full CRUD + list + FTS5 search coverage
- [ ] Epic parent-child relationships are supported via foreign keys with `parent_id`
- [ ] All operations are local-only (no network dependency)
- [ ] No non-abstract direct SQL calls leak into other packages (`@diricode/memory` boundary enforced)
- [ ] Issue schema includes: `id`, `title`, `description`, `status`, `priority`, `labels` (JSON), `parent_id`, `created_at`, `updated_at`
- [ ] FTS5 virtual table covers `title` and `description` columns with write-through sync

## Schema Sketch

```sql
CREATE TABLE issues (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  title TEXT NOT NULL,
  description TEXT,
  status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'open' CHECK(status IN ('open','in_progress','done','closed')),
  priority TEXT DEFAULT 'medium' CHECK(priority IN ('critical','high','medium','low')),
  labels TEXT DEFAULT '[]',  -- JSON array
  parent_id TEXT REFERENCES issues(id),
  created_at TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
  updated_at TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now'))
);

CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE issues_fts USING fts5(title, description, content=issues, content_rowid=rowid);
```

## Implementation Notes

### Mandatory Web Research Before Implementation

Research the following before starting:

1. **Local-first issue tracking systems** — Study `git-bug` ([github.com/git-bug/git-bug](https://github.com/git-bug/git-bug)), Fossil's built-in ticket system, and `sit` (Sit Issue Tracker) for local-first issue management patterns
2. **Repository pattern for SQLite** — Study how `better-sqlite3` projects structure repository classes with typed queries. See the Drizzle ORM repository pattern for SQLite
3. **FTS5 write-through triggers** — Study SQLite FTS5 documentation on content tables and triggers for keeping FTS index in sync with base table on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
4. **Parent-child hierarchies in SQLite** — Study recursive CTEs (`WITH RECURSIVE`) for epic → subtask tree traversal

### Reference Implementations

- **OpenAI Agents SDK** (`openai-agents-python`): Uses `AdvancedSQLiteSession` with SQLite for persistent state including message structure, branch management, and usage tracking. Schema includes `message_structure` table with foreign keys and `turn_usage` table — [OpenAI Agents Advanced SQLite Session docs](https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/sessions/advanced_sqlite_session/)
- **git-bug**: Distributed bug tracker embedded in git. Demonstrates local-first issue management with offline capability and optional sync to GitHub/GitLab/Jira — architecture validates the "local-first with sync adapters" approach
- **SQLite AI Ecosystem** (`sqliteai/sqlite-agent`): Shows how SQLite can serve as both state store and agent runtime, with extensions for FTS, vector search, and memory — validates SQLite as a capable foundation for agent issue state
- **Yodoca** (`VitalyOborin/yodoca`): Graph cognitive memory on SQLite with FTS5 + typed edges + temporal ordering — demonstrates advanced SQLite patterns for AI agent state

### Existing Code Context

- SQLite client already exists at `packages/memory/src/db/client.ts` (uses `better-sqlite3`, WAL mode enabled)
- Migration system exists at `packages/memory/src/db/migrations/`
- This issue adds a new migration for the `issues` table + FTS5 virtual table
- Must follow existing repository pattern in `packages/memory/src/`

## Dependencies

- **Upstream**: DC-MEM-001 (SQLite setup + migrations) — ✅ already implemented
- **Upstream**: DC-MEM-004 (FTS5 search) — FTS5 infrastructure should be established first
- **Downstream**: Pipeline uses issue system for sprint task management
- **Cross-epic**: `epic-pipeline` (task execution reads/writes issues)

## References

- `ADR-048` (SQLite Issue System — architectural rationale)
- `ADR-022` (superseded — old GitHub Issues approach)
- `docs/mvp/epic-memory.md` (canonical spec)
- `cross-cutting.md` (package boundary rules)

FTS5 virtual table over observation payload text. Ranked search returning session_id + excerpt + score. Incremental index sync on insert.

title: DC-MEM-003: Observation/timeline storage [MVP-1]
body:
**Epic**: #11 Memory and Project State Backbone [MVP-1]
**Iteration**: MVP-1
**Package**: `@diricode/memory`
**Spec**: `docs/mvp/epic-memory.md` → DC-MEM-003

## Goal

Implement timeline-based memory for agent/tool actions and decision traceability. Observations form the immutable audit trail of everything agents do — every file read, command run, decision made, and error encountered.

## Scope

- **`observations` table**:
  - `id`, `session_id`, `agent_id`, `task_id`, `type`, `content`, `timestamp`, `metadata` (JSON)
- **Observation type taxonomy (MVP)**:
  - `file_read`, `file_write`, `command_run`, `decision`, `discovery`, `error`
  - Validated on insert — invalid types rejected with typed error
- **Append/query API**:
  - `appendObservation(event)` — append-only insert (no updates/deletes)
  - `getTimeline(filters)` — fetch by session/task/agent/time-range with AND composition
  - Bulk export for context injection
- **Rich metadata**:
  - Tool name, file paths, command summaries, checkpoint IDs, guardrail signals
  - Stored as JSON in metadata column for extensibility
- **Event contract alignment**:
  - Events can be persisted and reconstructed in chronological order
  - Supports future context condenser and replay use-cases

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Observations are stored chronologically with stable monotonic timestamps
- [ ] All six MVP observation types are validated on insert and queryable
- [ ] Timeline queries support session-scoped, task-scoped, agent-scoped, and time-range filtered retrieval
- [ ] Metadata supports future context condenser and replay use-cases (extensible JSON)
- [ ] Invalid type/payload combinations fail with typed validation errors
- [ ] Append-only semantics enforced — no UPDATE or DELETE on observations
- [ ] Indexed for efficient time-range and session-scoped queries

## Schema Sketch

```sql
CREATE TABLE observations (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  session_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id),
  agent_id TEXT,
  task_id TEXT,
  type TEXT NOT NULL CHECK(type IN ('file_read','file_write','command_run','decision','discovery','error')),
  content TEXT NOT NULL,
  metadata TEXT DEFAULT '{}',  -- JSON: tool_name, file_paths, checkpoint_id, etc.
  timestamp TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now'))
);

CREATE INDEX idx_observations_session_time ON observations(session_id, timestamp);
CREATE INDEX idx_observations_type ON observations(type);
CREATE INDEX idx_observations_task ON observations(task_id);
```

## Implementation Notes

### Mandatory Web Research Before Implementation

Research the following before starting:

1. **Event sourcing for AI agents** — Study the append-only event store pattern described in [understandingdata.com/posts/event-sourcing-agents](https://understandingdata.com/posts/event-sourcing-agents/) — covers `EventLog` interface with `append()`, multi-agent timeline building, and SQLite as production single-node store
2. **Dual-contract persistence** — Study the Panex `protocol_events` + `storage_items` pattern ([medium.com/@impactarchitecture](https://medium.com/@impactarchitecture/persistence-model-for-a-dual-contract-event-store-in-sqlite-53f3505f7d21)) — append-only events table + mutable state table connected atomically in SQLite with WAL mode
3. **Graph cognitive memory** — Study Yodoca's (`VitalyOborin/yodoca`) node schema with `type`, `content`, `embedding`, `event_time`, `confidence`, `decay_rate`, `source_type`, `session_id` — demonstrates rich observation metadata in SQLite
4. **Observation type taxonomies** — Research how OpenTelemetry structures span types and how AI agent frameworks categorize action types

### Reference Implementations

- **Event Sourcing for Agents** (understandingdata.com): TypeScript `SqliteEventStore` with schema: `id`, `sequence_number`, `thread_id`, `type`, `category`, `payload`, `timestamp`, `parent_event_id`, `correlation_id`, `schema_version`. Demonstrates append-only with correlation IDs and schema versioning
- **Panex Event Store**: Dual-table SQLite design — `protocol_events` (append-only, monotonic ID, msgpack payload) + `storage_items` (mutable snapshot). WAL mode enables concurrent reads during writes. Validates our append-only observation + mutable state approach
- **Yodoca**: SQLite graph memory with episodic nodes that never decay (immutable audit trail), semantic/procedural/opinion nodes with confidence scoring. Single-writer async queue pattern prevents SQLite locking
- **LangChain/LangGraph**: State machine architecture where tool outputs accumulate in state. Session-scoped observation retrieval for context injection

### Existing Code Context

- Migration `001_observations.sql` already exists in `packages/memory/src/db/migrations/` — creates `observations` and `timeline_events` tables
- Verify existing migration schema matches this spec, extend if needed
- Follow append-only pattern — repository class should NOT expose update/delete methods for observations
- Align observation types with tool names from `packages/tools/src/` (bash, file-read, file-write, grep, glob)

## Dependencies

- **Upstream**: DC-MEM-001 (SQLite + migrations) — ✅ implemented
- **Upstream**: DC-MEM-002 (sessions) — observations reference `session_id` FK
- **Downstream**: DC-MEM-004 (FTS5 search indexes observation content)
- **Cross-epic**: `epic-observability` (timeline events power agent tree + metrics)
- **Cross-epic**: `epic-pipeline` (checkpoints reference observation IDs)

## References

- `docs/mvp/epic-memory.md` (canonical spec)
- `analiza-context-management.md` (timeline-first memory recommendation)
- `ankieta-features-ekosystem.md` (timeline memory decision)
- `overview.md` (observability + memory linkage)

title: DC-MEM-002: Session storage [MVP-1]
body:
**Epic**: #11 Memory and Project State Backbone [MVP-1]
**Iteration**: MVP-1
**Package**: `@diricode/memory`
**Spec**: `docs/mvp/epic-memory.md` → DC-MEM-002

## Goal

Persist session and message history as first-class entities for replay, analysis, and pipeline continuity. Sessions are the primary unit of interaction tracking — every user conversation creates a session containing an ordered sequence of messages with token/cost telemetry.

## Scope

- **`sessions` table**:
  - `id`, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `status` (enum: created/active/completed/archived), `metadata` (JSON)
- **`messages` table**:
  - `id`, `session_id`, `role` (user/assistant/system/tool), `content`, `tokens`, `cost`, `agent_id`, `timestamp`
  - FK: `messages.session_id → sessions.id` with CASCADE delete
  - Indexed lookups by session and time
- **Repository/API methods**:
  - Session CRUD: create, read, update status/metadata, list with filters
  - Message CRUD: append + retrieval per session
  - Soft-close semantics (MVP preference — mark as completed, don't delete)
- **Pagination and ordering**:
  - Chronological retrieval (ASC default for replay)
  - Cursor-based pagination for large sessions
  - Bounded page size

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Sessions can be created, updated, listed, and fetched by ID
- [ ] Messages can be appended and retrieved per session in deterministic chronological order
- [ ] Deleting/closing sessions uses soft-close (status update, not row deletion)
- [ ] Session/message queries are indexed and performant for typical MVP workloads
- [ ] API returns typed, schema-validated entities (Zod validation at boundaries)
- [ ] Message schema tracks `tokens` and `cost` per message for telemetry integration
- [ ] Session status transitions are validated (no invalid state changes)

## Schema Sketch

```sql
CREATE TABLE sessions (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'created' CHECK(status IN ('created','active','completed','archived')),
  metadata TEXT DEFAULT '{}',  -- JSON
  created_at TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
  updated_at TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now'))
);

CREATE TABLE messages (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  session_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  role TEXT NOT NULL CHECK(role IN ('user','assistant','system','tool')),
  content TEXT NOT NULL,
  tokens INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
  cost REAL DEFAULT 0.0,
  agent_id TEXT,
  timestamp TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now'))
);

CREATE INDEX idx_messages_session_time ON messages(session_id, timestamp);
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_status ON sessions(status);
```

## Implementation Notes

### Mandatory Web Research Before Implementation

Research the following before starting:

1. **AI agent session storage patterns** — Study how OpenAI Agents SDK stores sessions in SQLite (`AdvancedSQLiteSession` with `agent_sessions` + `agent_messages` + `message_structure` tables). See [OpenAI Agents Advanced SQLite docs](https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/sessions/advanced_sqlite_session/)
2. **Cursor-based pagination in SQLite** — Study keyset pagination (`WHERE timestamp > ? ORDER BY timestamp ASC LIMIT ?`) vs offset pagination for message retrieval
3. **Session state machines** — Study how frameworks like Semantic Kernel and Microsoft Agent Framework handle per-turn state persistence (load at turn start, persist at turn end)
4. **Deduplication patterns** — Study the Hermes Agent bug ([NousResearch/hermes-agent#860](https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/860)) where multiple write paths caused 3-4x message duplication in SQLite — implement single-writer pattern to avoid this

### Reference Implementations

- **OpenAI Agents SDK**: `AdvancedSQLiteSession` — stores sessions with branching support, per-turn token usage tracking (`turn_usage` table), and message structure metadata. Production-quality SQLite schema with proper FK constraints and indexes
- **Hermes Agent** (`NousResearch/hermes-agent`): Demonstrates pitfalls of multiple write paths to same SQLite session DB — critical lesson on deduplication and single-writer queues
- **Microsoft Agent Framework**: Instantiation-per-request model where state loads at turn start and persists at turn end. Turn timeout defaults. State keyed by conversation ID — validates our session-scoped approach
- **Yodoca** (`VitalyOborin/yodoca`): Single-writer async queue pattern for SQLite writes avoids "database is locked" under concurrent access. Episodic nodes as immutable audit trail

### Existing Code Context

- SQLite client at `packages/memory/src/db/client.ts` with `better-sqlite3` + WAL mode
- Existing migrations in `packages/memory/src/db/migrations/` — this adds a new migration
- Message `tokens`/`cost` columns align with DC-MEM-005 (token usage tracking)
- Follow existing repository class pattern in `packages/memory/src/`

## Dependencies

- **Upstream**: DC-MEM-001 (SQLite setup + migrations) — ✅ already implemented
- **Downstream**: DC-PIPE-001 (turn lifecycle stores turn metadata in sessions)
- **Cross-epic**: `epic-observability` (session data powers metrics)

## References

- `docs/mvp/epic-memory.md` (canonical spec)
- `spec-mvp-diricode.md` (session lifecycle and memory ownership)
- `analiza-context-management.md` (timeline and context persistence patterns)

title: DC-PROV-003: Error classifier
body:
**Epic**: #3 Provider Router [POC → MVP-1]
**Iteration**: MVP-1

Central error normalization: rate_limited, context_overflow, overloaded, quota_exhausted, auth_error, not_found, other. Structured shape: {kind, provider, model, retryable, retryAfterMs, raw}.
